coredata.dc:title: Application of Cys A@AuNPs supported amino acids towards rapid and selective identification of Hg(II) and Cu(II) ions in aqueous solution: An innovative microfluidic paper-based (μPADs) colorimetric sensing platform
coredata.dc:creator: Abdollahiyan, Parinaz
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Pashazadeh-Panahi, Paria
Seidi, Farzad
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an innovative methods was proposed for the synthesis of cysteamine@gold nanoparticle (CysA@AuNPs) using one step protocol. Also, application of CysA@AuNPs for the sensitive recognition of Hg (II) and Cu (II) in aqueous media was investigated using optical techniques. This research work explains the development of a lightweight stamp for manufacturing of a novel microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPADs), as a simple colorimetric sensor for selective recognition of Hg2+ and Cu2+ within the other metal ions (Fe(III), Mn(II), Zr(IV), Ba(II), Mg(II), Sr(II), As(III), B(III), Al(III), V(III), K(I), Na(I), Mo(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Sn(II), Se(IV), Cr(III), Bi(III)) in aqueous, and human biofluids. CysA@AuNPs supported amino acids were used as the ion detection probs. The specific colorimetric response of Hg2+ and Cu2+ amongst the other ions through the innovative sensor allows naked-eye discrimination of the mercury and copper metal ions in this work with excellent selectivity in the presence of cysteine, and methionine. The results revealed our new strategy (μPADs) for the colorimetric recognition of mercury and copper ions, in both urine and aqueous samples, is selective, simple, fast, sensitive, quantitative, cost-effective, and eco-friendly, which can be detected by naked eye through color alternation with LLOQ of 0.001 ppm for Hg2+, and 0.02 ppm for Cu2+ with admirable stability. The low instrumental needs of the stamp, and disposability of μPADs enable the application of the suggested paper-based device for the commercial diagnostics biosensor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Materials and equipment (I), and the process (II) of µPADs fabrication. Melt the paraffin of the local candles under 90 °C, following by moothly placed the filter paper through it (A), and leaved it for 2 s (B), afterwards, the filter paper was taken out (C) to prepare paraffinated paper (D). The locally machined steel stamp was heated on 150 °C for 2 s, and the p-paper was put on the n-paper (E), before stamping (F). The stamping step was performed during 8 s (G) to prepare the proposed µPADs.

Microfluidic paper-based (μPADs) colorimetric sensor, molding by a local-made hot template on the paraffin-coated paper (p-paper), through which, heat may transfer to the native paper (n-paper). The AuotoCAD dimensions of the paper (I). P-paper is put on the n-paper (A), a local made stainless steel template was prepared with dimensions d = 5 mm for the peripheral circles, d = 10 mm for the central circle, 10 mm for the length of the channels, and 3 mm for the width of the channels (B), The template was stamped on the papers (in contact with the p-paper) with 2 kg in pressure through magnet (C), the µPADs were prepared (D), and used as the colorimetric sensor for specific detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+(E) (II).

AuNps-CysA supported different amino acids (glycine, valine, proline, methionine, and cysteine) (10 mM) for Cu2+ detection with different ratios (15 µl, 50 µl, and 100 µl) in 100 µl of AuNPs-CysA, and 50 µl of amino acid additives.
Colorimetric sensing of different ions (Sr, Ni, Hg, Mn, As, K, Co, Zr, Li) by CysA-AuNPs in the presence of cysteine as supporting agent. (AA refers to amino acid).
Corresponding effects of Cu2+ and Hg2+ (1 µg/ml) (225 µl) in cysteine (10 mM) (100 µl) and AuNPs-CysA (200 µl).

Colorimetric sensing of the interactions between AuNPs-capped cysteamine, in the presence of cysteine (10 mM) for detection of different ions (Mg2+, Mo2+, B3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, As3+, Se4+, Cr3+, Bi3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, V3+, Cs+, Zr4+, Te4+, K+, Li2+, Al3+, Na+, Si4+, and Mn2+, Fe3+), using standard solutions with 1 µg/ml concentration. The ratios are: ions (100 µl), cysteine amino acid (50 µl), and AuNPs-CysA (100 µl).

Colorimetric sensing of the interactions between AuNPs-capped cysteamine, in the presence of methionine (10 mM) for detection of different ions (Mg2+, Mo2+, B3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, As3+, Se4+, Cr3+, Bi3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, V3+, Cs+, Zr4+, W4+, K+, Li2+, Al3+, Na+, Si4+, and Mn2+, Fe3+), using standard solutions with 1 µg/ml concentration. Regarding to evaluate the effects of the Cu2+ ratios of the colorimetric sensing by AuNPs-CysA supported methionine, low ratio (15 µl) and high ratio (100 µl) of Cu2+ was applied to 100 µl of AuNP-CysA, and 50 µl of methionine (10 mM).

The UV–vis spectra of the interaction between CysA-AuNPs supported methionine (10 mM) for detection of different ions (Fe3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ti2+, Cu2+, and Li2+), using standard solutions in aqueous sample (1 µg/ml) (A), and the histogram of the absorption intensity vs. types on ions (B). The ratios are 200 µl AuNPs, 100 µl methionine, and 15 µl ions.

Colorimetric sensing of the interaction between AuNPs-capped cysteamine, in the presence of ascorbic acid (1 M) and various amino acids (1 mM) (cysteine, Leucine, l-arginine, alanine, methionine, glycine, and valine), for detection of different ions (Sr2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Te4+, As2+, K+, Co2+, Zr4+, and Li+), using standard solutions. As illustrated in the sensing arrays of different amino acids, it is obvious that in the presence of cysteine, the Hg2+ ion can be detected via AuNP-cysteamine probe.

The UV–vis spectra of the interaction between CysA-AuNPs supported cysteine (1 mM) for detection of different ions (Sr2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Te4+, As3+, K+, Co2+, and Zr4+) , using standard solutions in aqueous sample (A), and the histogram of the absorption intensity vs. types on ions (B). The graph of the absorption value is ploted at 520 nm.
Selectivity of Hg2+ amongst the other metal ions in the presence of CysA-AuNPs solution (A-C) and with ratios of 225 μl for ions, 125 μl of CysA-AuNPs solution, and 50 μl of cysteine (D, and E)..

The UV–vis spectra of the interaction between CysA-AuNPs supported cysteine (10 mM) for detection of different ions (Fe3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Li2+, and Cu2+), using standard solutions in aqueous sample (1 µg/ml) (A), and the histogram of the absorption intensity vs. types on ions (B).

UV–Vis spectra of CysA-AuNPs supported cysteine (10 mM) in the presence of different volumes of Cu2+ (15, 50, and 100 μl). UV–Vis spectra (A), the histogram of the absorption intensity vs. volume of Cu2+ (B).

UV–Vis spectra of CysA-AuNPs supported cysteine (10 mM) in the presence of various concentrations of Cu2+ (0.001, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 1 μg/ml). UV–Vis spectra (A), the histogram of the absorption intensity vs. concentration of Cu2+ (B).
Fluorescence spectrometry of CysA, AuNPs, and CysA-AuNPs. The excitation wavelength of AuNP-CysA is considered at 520 nm.

Fluorescence spectrometry of different ions in CysA-AuNPs supported cysteine (10 mM) in water sample (A), and their relevant intensity fluorescence peak (B). The excitation wavelength of AuNP-CysA is considered at 520 nm.
Visual color change of CysA-AuNPs modified with cysteine in the presence of various concentrations of Hg2+ (0.001, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 1 μg/ml) in three repetitions.

Colorimetric sensing of the interactions between AuNPs-capped cysteamine, in the presence of cysteine (10 mM) for detection of different ions (Mg2+, Mo2+, B3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, As3+, Se4+, Cr3+, Bi3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, V3+, Cs+, Zr4+, Fe3+, K+, Li2+, Al3+, Na+, Si4+, and Mn2+, Te4+), using standard solutions with 1 µg/ml concentration in human urine sample. (100 µl) of ions were applied to 100 µl of AuNP-CysA, and 50 µl of cysteine (10 mM).

Colorimetric sensing of the interactions between AuNPs-CysA, in the presence of methionine (10 mM) for detection of different ions (Mg2+, Mo2+, B3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, As3+, Se4+, Cr3+, Bi3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, V3+, Cs+, Zr4+, Fe3+, K+, Li2+, Al3+, Na+, Si4+, and Mn2+, Te4+), using standard solutions with 1 µg/ml concentration in human urine sample. (100 µl) of ions were applied to 100 µl of AuNP-CysA, and 50 µl of methionine (10 mM).

Schematic illustration of suggested CysA-AuNPs, amino acids, and metal ions interplays: (a) No Interactions are revealed, (b) CysA-AuNPs are aggregated by metal ions, (c) Metal ions interact with amino acids and avoid CysA-AuNPs aggregations; and (d) The aggregation of CysA-AuNPs are induced by co-contribution of metal ions and amino acids.

In the absence of the metal ions, CysA-AuNPs can be agglomerated as the influence of cysteine molecules with low concentration of 1 mM. Interaction between amine groups of CysA-AuNPs with carboxylic acid groups of cysteine-cysteine complexes cause to CysA-AuNPs accumulation (A). In the presence of both cysteine amino acid, and the positively-charged metal ions (Hg2+ is not included in this group, since its thiophilic nature), the CysA-AuNPs might be affected under double agglomeration effects of the cysteine molecules, and the positively-charged ions through chemical bonds, and electrostatic attractions, respectively, causing to different CysA-AuNPs agglomeration rate with that for the previous group (B). In the presence of Hg2+ ions, supporting cysteine, CysA-AuNPs might be detached from cysteine molecules, owing to the high thiophilicity of cysteine, and being deagglomerated, leading to occur cysteine-Hg2+ binds. Further, Hg2+ molecules might have tendency to interact with the free electron-rich nitrogen of the facial amine groups of CysA-AuNPs via N-Hg2+-N binds, causing to partial agglomeration of CysA-AuNPs. The rate of agglomeration in this group is different with the previous ones (C).

AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM); some of the cysteamine molecules lose their binds with AuNPs in the presence of cysteine (10 mM) to form cysteine-cysteamine binds, and the free AuNPs are assembled under the electrostatic effects with the free electron-rich functional groups in the system (A). Hg2+ ions in the presence of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) can interact with the thiol and amine groups in the system, and caused to AuNPs aggregation, and therefore, the clustered AuNPs are sediment (B). AuNPs-CysA supported methionine; methionine molecules can bind with AuNP-CysA through electrostatic interactions between amine groups of AuNP-CysA and the carboxylic groups of methionine (C). In the presence of Cu2+, cysteine molecules are separated from AuNP-CysA complex and make chelating rings for Cu2+, where the AuNPs are dispersed throughout the system under the influence of the electrostatic repulsions (D). In the presence of Cu2+, methionine molecules are separated from AuNP-CysA complex and make chelating rings for Cu2+, where the AuNPs are dispersed throughout the system under the influence of the electrostatic repulsions (E).
Recent analytical strategies for Hg2+ recognition.

head.ce:title: Application of Cys A@AuNPs supported amino acids towards rapid and selective identification of Hg(II) and Cu(II) ions in aqueous solution: An innovative microfluidic paper-based (μPADs) colorimetric sensing platform

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an innovative methods was proposed for the synthesis of cysteamine@gold nanoparticle (CysA@AuNPs) using one step protocol. Also, application of CysA@AuNPs for the sensitive recognition of Hg (II) and Cu (II) in aqueous media was investigated using optical techniques. This research work explains the development of a lightweight stamp for manufacturing of a novel microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPADs), as a simple colorimetric sensor for selective recognition of Hg2+ and Cu2+ within the other metal ions (Fe(III), Mn(II), Zr(IV), Ba(II), Mg(II), Sr(II), As(III), B(III), Al(III), V(III), K(I), Na(I), Mo(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Sn(II), Se(IV), Cr(III), Bi(III)) in aqueous, and human biofluids. CysA@AuNPs supported amino acids were used as the ion detection probs. The specific colorimetric response of Hg2+ and Cu2+ amongst the other ions through the innovative sensor allows naked-eye discrimination of the mercury and copper metal ions in this work with excellent selectivity in the presence of cysteine, and methionine. The results revealed our new strategy (μPADs) for the colorimetric recognition of mercury and copper ions, in both urine and aqueous samples, is selective, simple, fast, sensitive, quantitative, cost-effective, and eco-friendly, which can be detected by naked eye through color alternation with LLOQ of 0.001 ppm for Hg2+, and 0.02 ppm for Cu2+ with admirable stability. The low instrumental needs of the stamp, and disposability of μPADs enable the application of the suggested paper-based device for the commercial diagnostics biosensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals, owing to their bioaccumulation persistence and toxicity, compromise the natural world and their accelerate pollution levels leading to great concerns [1]. The effects of these toxic materials are very harmful and affect the entire ecosystem. Chief among all, mercury ion (Hg2+) is the most toxic pollutant for the environment [2]. The soils, sediments, and bodies of water are exposed to Hg2+ contamination which negatively affects animal, plant, and consequently, humans' health [3]. Moreover, the accumulation of this toxic metal throughout the food chain drives hazardous health consequences such as brain damage, kidney failure, neuromuscular effects, and troubles in the digestive system. Besides, carcinogenic activities are other serious effects of the mercury ion [4]. Thus, it is imperative to assess and monitor the levels of Hg2+ to diminish the hazardous risks of this toxic pollutant in human and other creatures' health. Despite several studies on detecting the levels of mercury ions in the biological environments, a lot of gray areas have still remained to be colored in [5,6]. The traditional techniques for Hg2+ and some of other toxic ions and dyes detection are including atomic fluorescence spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, fluorescence method, and plasma mass spectrometry [7-18]. Colorimetric methods have provided a rapid, simple, cost-effective, and sensitive approach for real-time and on-time monitoring of metal ions, in particular, aqueous Hg2+ [19].

Copper (Cu2+) is the other pivotal metal ion that act a vital role in structure of red blood cell and hemoglobin as well as energy metabolism cycle [20]. One of the chief disorders associated with copper is Wilson. Regarding the importance of copper ion, in industry and the biomedical fields, expansion of sensitive and accurate detection and determination methods is in the central point of attention [20].

Recently, µPADs have demonstrated high attraction to be used in a variety of bioanalytical diagnostic and sensing sensors [21–23]. µPADs fabrication usually needs hydrophobic barriers on the substrate to specify the fluid flows regions [24–26] The hydrophilic nature of paper leads to induce the lateral flow action, and in consequent, the fluidic transport within the porous structure of the paper [27]. Hydrophobic barriers can be created by various techniques such as wax printing photolithography [28], laser cutting [29], screen-printing [30], and plotting of poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) [31]. The application of paper-based sensors in clinical tests has been studied in association with various detection systems such as mass spectrometry [32], colorimetric [33], chemiluminescence [34], and electrochemical assays [35]. Colorimetric-based assay has been one of the most favorable detection systems owing to admirable instrumental advantages such as portability and ease of detection by naked eye [36–38].

In this research study, we proposed a novel µPADs with no needs to any color-modifier reagents. Our µPADs works on the base of solution absorbance to the paper, such that the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs can rapidly adsorb to the paper in the presence of Hg2+ and Cu2+, but the case is not the same for the other types of ions or for amino acids alone. Moreover, in this study, for the first time we employed CysA-AuNPs to detect Hg2+ and Cu2+ amongst other metal ions, selectively, resulting in noticeable alternations in optical and color properties, which was further confirmed through fluorescence spectroscopy and UV radiation. Besides, the regular morphology and narrow size distribution is also imperative to repeat assessment results. Therefore, appropriate reducing agents and capped agents are useful for synthesis of shape controllable and monodisperse AuNPs, which is participate to raise the reproducibility and sensitivity of Hg2+. The specific colorimetric answer of Hg2+ and Cu2+ amongst the other ions through the innovative sensor (color alternations in each of amino acid and AuNP couples) allows naked-eye discrimination of the mercury and copper metal ions in this work with excellent selectivity in the presence of cysteine. In low concentration of cysteine (1 mM), since the thiol group of the cysteamine is involved with AuNP interaction, the electron-riches amino groups are free, providing the chance for interacting with carboxylic acid groups of cysteine-cysteine complexes. Thus, the CysA-AuNPs can be accumulated through NH2 – COOH- interactions of cysteamine-cysteine, in the absence of Hg2+. In high concentration of cysteine (10 mM), the addition of cysteine caused to cysteamine detachment from some of the AuNPs, and making cysteine-cysteamine complexes. The intact Au-CysA might be interacted with the cysteine-cysteamine complexes by electrostatic interplays between the carboxyl groups of the cysteine-cysteamine complexes and the amine groups of AuNP-CysA. Moreover, the free AuNPs are assembled around the free amine groups through electrostatic repulsions and dispersed throughout the solution. These interactions between cysteine (in both concentrations) and CysA-AuNPs caused to change the color of the CysA-AuNPs solution from wine red to royal pink that can be detected by naked eye. Despite the chemical interplays between CysA-AuNPs and cysteine amino acid, their fluorometry spectrum approximately overlapped with each other, owing to their negative charges which causes to interparticle repulsion and prevent the fluorescence quenching. After adding Hg2+, ion-cysteine interactions might occur due to not only electrostatic interactions between the positively-charged Hg2+ and the negatively-charged cysteine, but also high thiophilicity of cysteine which causes to defeat the NH2 – COOH- interactions of cysteamine-cysteine, and make Hg2+-cysteine complex, leading to partial deagglomeration of CysA-AuNPs. However, the process of deagglomeration was not completely occurred for CysA-AuNPs, because of the partial interactions between the positively charged Hg2+ and the negatively charged CysA-AuNPs, leading to partial agglomeration of CysA-AuNPs. Further, the colorimetric sensing can detect the color alternation of the CysA-AuNPs supported cysteine solution from royal purple to ruby pink in the presence of Hg2+, which can be detected by naked eye. Moreover, the intensity of the fluorometric spectrum of the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs was increased in the presence of Hg2+, due to the partial de-agglomeration of the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs by Hg2+ molecules. Since cysteine is a chelating agent for Cu2+, cysteine molecules lose their interactions with AuNPs-CysA or with cysteamine molecules in the solution to form chelating ring for Cu2+. Therefore Cu2+ is chelated through thiol and amine binds of cysteine, which is accompanied by color alternation of the solution from pink to ruby red, and increment in the fluorometry and UV–vis peak intensity, in the presence of Cu2+. In the presence of the other ions, except Hg2+ and Cu2+, CysA-AuNPs agglomeration can be triggered even more than the cysteine supported probs without ions. The reason is regarded to the double and simultaneous effects of the positively charged ions, as well as cysteine on the aggregation of CysA-AuNPs, leading to change the color of the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs solution from royal purple to blue. Moreover, the intensity of the fluorometric spectrum of the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs was decreased in the presence of positively charged metal ions, except for Hg2+, due to the agglomeration of the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs by these molecules. On μPADs, the partially aggregated CysA-cysteine, which possess lower dimensions can path through the TLC paper substrate, while the completely agglomerated CysA-cysteine in the presence of the cysteine, and other metal ions, except Hg2+ cannot easily go through the paper, and remained unabsorbed on the substrate. Thus, Hg2+ and Cu2+ can be easily detected on the designed μPADs in this research work.

To the best of our knowledge, the proposed technique for selective Hg2+ and Cu2+ detection amongst 20 different metal ions in aqueous, human urine sample, and on paper-based μPADs is completely innovative and was not previously reported. The results revealed our new technique for the colorimetric recognition of mercury and copper ions, in both urine and aqueous samples, is selective, simple, fast, sensitive, quantitative, cost-effective, and eco-friendly, which can be detected by naked eye through color alternation with admirable stability. Moreover, we proposed to apply amino acids, human biological substances, to recognize Hg2+ and Cu2+ with the sensitivity of 0.001 ppm in aqueous solutions, which is even lower than the FDA-approved amount of Hg2+ (0.001 ppm) in nature and biological environments. Thus, the proposed sensing probe can be efficiently useful for preventing the diseases which are raised from the Hg2+ and Cu2+ toxic effects in both environment and human body. In addition, the enhanced color uniformity, the low instrumental needs of the stamp, and disposability of μPADs enable the application of the suggested paper-based device for the commercial diagnostics biosensor.

All of the used chemicals were of the highest purity or analytical grade. The standard solutions of (Hg(II), Fe(III), Mn(II), Zr(IV), Ba(II), Mg(II), Sr(II), As(III), B(III), Al(III), V(III), K(I), Na(I), Mo(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Sn(II), Se(IV), Cr(III), Bi(III)) (with 1 μg/ml concentration) were purchased from chemlab company (Zedelgem, Belgium). Tetrachloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H20) (99%), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) ( ≥ 99.0%), Cysteamine (95%), L-cysteine (97%), l(+)-Ascorbic acid (AA) (99%), Leucine ( ≥ 98%), l-arginine ( ≥ 98%), alanine ( ≥ 98%), methionine ( ≥ 99%), glycine ( ≥ 99%), and valine( ≥ 98%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich co.(Germany). The human plasma samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran). For all dilutions deionized water (pH = 7.4) was used. Paraffin was acquired from local market.

Briefly, 50 mL of HAuCl4 (0.5 mM) was heated under magnetic stirrer and reflux states until boiling point. Subsequently, 5 mL of sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) solution (38.8 mM) was mixed with the prepared solution, during which, the color of the solution altered from yellow into wine-red. The magnetically stirring was continued until the obtained wine-red color was fixed. Afterwards, to functionalize citrate treated-Au NPs with CysA, CysA was gradually mixed with AuNPs with ratio of 100:1 and stirring for 12 h. The final color of CysA-AuNPs solution is dark red color with the stability up to couple of months at refrigerator condition (4 °C) [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of colorimetric ion sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric identifications of ions were performed at room temperature. Briefly, ascorbic acid and different amino acids (arginine, alanine, cysteine, glycine, Leucine, methionine, valine) were diluted with deionized water, human urine sample, separately, to obtain 1 mM , and 10 mM concentrations of each amino acids, and combined with 1 mM of optical probe (CysA-AuNPs) with the ratio of (1:3, amino acid: CysA-AuNP). Afterwards, the standard solutions of ions (Hg(II), Fe(III), Mn(II), Zr(IV), Ba(II), Mg(II), Sr(II), As(III), B(III), Al(III), V(III), K(I), Na(I), Mo(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Sn(II), Se(IV), Cr(III), Bi(III)) (with 1 μg/ml concentration) were added to the mixture solution of amino acid with CysA-AuNPs in the separate microtubes, either in high concentration of ions (225 μl of ions, and 175 μl of Au-amino acid media), or low concentrations of ions (100 μl of ions, and 175 μl of Au-amino acid media).

The construction of the μPADs was performed as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 , and Fig. 2 . Firstly, the appropriate paper sheet was soaked into melt paraffin (at 90 °C) for 60 s. Then, the paper was removed from the paraffin, maintain to solidify at ambient temperature and then settled on the n-paper surface. A steel stamp (fabricated by a local shop, Tabriz, Iran) was preheated on a hot plate at 150 °C for 120 s and stamp on the p-paper to prepare microfluidic structure with hydrophobic barriers on the n-paper. In fact, applying 2 Kg pressure on the paper for 2 s lead to thermal transfer to the n-paper through the paraffin. μPADs were patterned in a geometry based on eight circular recognition zones for microfluidic biodevices including one central circular zone (d = 10 mm), connected to the 8 surrounded detecting zones (d = 5 mm) through internal channels with 3 mm in width, and 10 mm in length. The ultimate dimensions of the fabricated μPADs were 50 mm × 50 mm (Fig. 2).
Colorimetric identification was carried out with the camera (Samsung smart phone, 600-dpi resolution). The images were recorded under natural or flasher light sources.

The fluorescent detection of the studied ions was carried out at room temperature using cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs. In a typical run, 1 mM of cysteine was mixed with standard solution of different ions (Sr2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Te4+, As2+, K+, Co2+, Zr4+, and Li2+) (1 ppm) with the ratio of 125
μ l
of CysA-AuNPs, 50
μ l
of cysteine, and 225
μ l
of ions, or withe ratio of 125
μ l
of CysA-AuNPs, 50
μ l
of cysteine, and 100
μ l
of ions and equilibrated at room temperature for 5 min, following by exposing to the fluorescence evaluation on the fluorescence spectrophotometer, repeating twice for each sample, the excitation wavelength was considered at 520 nm.

The size and morphology of the CysA-AuNSs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by a Hitachi H-800 TEM device operated at a voltage of 200 kV. The spectra of absorption were recorded through UV–vis spectroscopy, at room temperature, using a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Infrared spectra were recorded by a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometers. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was attained on a Multimode 8 force microscope (Bruker, Germany). Zeta potential tests were performed on a PALS zeta potential analyzer, Brookhaven Instruments Corp, ver.3.54. The fluorescence spectroscopy assay was performed by a Lengguang Tech F96 pro fluorescence spectrophotometer device (Lengguang Tech, Shanghai, China). Besides, fluorescence images were captured with a Canon 7Ds digital camera (Canon, Tokyo, Japan) applying a Qinke ZF-7A UV lamp as the light source (Qinke, Shanghai, China). The morphology of the AuNPs, and CysA-AuNPs were assessed with a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis, conducting on FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic).

Various kinds of substances such as carbon-dots, metal nanoparticles (e.g., AuNPs [40], AgNPs) [41], and CuNPs [42]), and semiconductor quantum dots (semi-QDs) [43] show unique optical features and have been applied for colorimetric sensing in several applications such as biological, and environmental usages. For instance, researchers synthesized AgNPs through a wet-chemical reduction process where γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), and citrate (Cit) were applied as stabilizers. The GABA-Cit@AgNPs sensing solution could recognize Hg2+ in environmental samples through colorimetric detection with LOD of 2.37 μM, and a linear range detection of 5–35 μM [44]. In another study, the hazardous mercury ion was recognized by nitrogen co-doped carbon dots sensing solution which was synthesized through the chemical interactions between citric acid and melamine, under thermal condition. The prepared nitrogen co-doped carbon dots prob solution could recognize Hg2+ in the linear range of 2 to 14 μM, and LOD of 0.44 μM, using human milk as real sample [45].

Chief among all, AuNPs are more favorable due to excellent performances as follows. AuNPs show superior photostability than AgNPs, and are desirable for imaging, and photothermal therapy in living organisms [46]. On the other hand, AgNPs lead to mild inflammatory response, and cytotoxicity [47]. Semi-QDs are toxic because of their poor solubility, besides use of bivalent, and tetravalent semiconductors such as PbSe, CdSe, and CdS [48,49]. Notably, AuNPs can be simply fabricated and stabilized via sodium citrate in one step. Though, the synthesis procedures of carbon dots, CuNPs, AgNPs, and semi-QDs are more complicated [50,51]. Moreover, the synthesis condition of AgNPs need to be controlled, regarding to the impacts of the Ag+ reduction rate. If the reduction rate of Ag ion is too fast, AgNPs aggregate, and, if it is too slow, Ag2O is gained. CuNPs are less stable and more reactive in aqueous solution, in room temperature, than that of AgNPs and AuNPs [52]. Therefore, CuNPs synthesis is carried out under inert atmosphere. Carbon-dots, and Semi-QDs preparations are tedious because extra multi-steps are performed such as carbonization, reflux under N2 atmosphere, centrifugation, and dialysis [53,54].

Owing to their tiny sizes, AuNPs have several unique physicochemical features, such as high electron densities, admirable catalytic performance, optical characteristics and biocompatibility. Their fluorescence quenching, and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) are the most useful optical properties. Colorimetric sensing of AuNPs is based on their admirable optical properties and the color alternations resulting from AuNPs aggregation and their interparticle distances or morphologies changes [55]. Hence, in accordance with the previous studies [56,57], AuNPs are desirable materials for Hg2+ detection due to simple synthesis procedure, non-toxicity, and photostability, compare with the other materials. Previously, researchers found that L-cysteine can accumulate citrate stabilized AuNPs through electrostatic interactions between thiol groups of cysteine molecules and the amine groups of citrate. The presence of Cu2+ caused to create disulfide cysteine, where two cysteine molecules can link with each other by thiol-thiol reactions. Therefore, the aggregation of AuNPs supported cysteine could be affected in the presence of Cu2+, leading to the color alternation of the sensing solution. The LOD of the colorimetric detection of Cu2+ in this approach was 10 nM [58]. In another study, researchers synthesized methionine-capped gold nanoclusters with unique optical, electrical, and physical properties, such as intense yellow fluorescence, and long fluorescence lifetime, large Stoke's shift, and high colloidal stability. Therefore, they found that the methionine-capped AuNPs were promising material for fluorometric detection of Cu2+ with linear response of 50 nM to 8 µM, and LOD of 7.9 nM [59].

However, to the best of our knowledge, our super-sensitive and ultra-selective system for Hg2+ and Cu2+ detection has not previously reported. Since to their surface characters, the Au NPs do not address the needs for biological or chemical sensing. Hence, their surfaces require to be treated with functional groups with binding capabilities, surface activities, and high selectivity to enhance the nanoparticle compatibility and dispersity, the strategies of AuNPs surface modification methods are revealed in Scheme 1 .

Several amino acids including glycine, valine, proline, methionine, and cysteine were examined for Cu2+, and Hg2+ detection. The color alternations were occurred for Hg2+ by AuNP-CysA supported cysteine and Cu2+ by AuNP-CysA supported cysteine and methionine (Fig. 3 ).

The reason for Hg2+ detection in the presence of cysteine is related to the presence of thiol group in cysteine. Cysteine was examined for colorimetric detection of ions in two concentrations of 1 mM (low concentration), and 10 mM (high concentration). Low concentration of cysteine cannot break the cysteamine and AuNPs linkages, though, high concentration of cysteine cause to ruin the binds between cysteamine and AuNPs in some of AuNP-CysA, in a cysteine-concentration dependent manner, and form new binds between cysteine and cysteamine via thiol-thiol interactions. This cysteine-cysteamine bind might be ruined in the presence of Hg2+ and Cu2+ under the effects of the ionic interactions. In fact, methionine and cysteine are chelator for Cu2+, which can make chelating rings with Cu2+ via thiol and amine binds of cysteine, or carboxyl and amine binds of methionine. However, in the presence of the other amino acids (non-thiol contained amino acids), the AuNP-CysA can be accumulated in the presence of ions through electrostatic interactions between the positively charged ions and the negatively charged AuNP-CysA supported amino acids particles. In the lower concentrations of cysteine, two cysteine molecules are interacted with each other via their terminal thiol groups and two AuNPs-CysA might be interacted with the cysteine couple through electrostatic interactions between two carboxyl groups of cysteine couple and the amine groups of AuNPs-CysA, leading to AuNPs-CysA agglomeration in the presence of low concentrated cysteine (1 Mm).

According to the procedure of the colorimetric sensing based on CysA-AuNPs aggregation by cysteine, the produced AuNPs was obtained by a sodium citrate reduction approach, where the citrate ions played role both as a stabilizer and a reductant. The citrate ions with negative charges can surround AuNPs, offering strong electrostatic repulsion, causing to stability of AuNPs. The color of AuNPs appeared in wine-red, dedicating the state of the particles dispersion and showed the maximum absorbance at around 520 nm, associating to the unique SPR of AuNPs. Subsequently, cysteamine was applied as an amine group stabilizer to provide a nanoprobe for ions detection. In fact, the thiol group of cysteamine interacts with the AuNPs surface via Au-S binds. The NH2 - or NH3 + group of cysteamine, depending on the pH of the solution, is in exposure to the AuNPs surface, which can react with amino acids. In agreement with the previous studies [60,61], this result showed the -SH group alongside AuNPs in Au-S bonds. The CysA-AuNPs is stabilized owing to the cysteamine NH3 + group electrostatic repulsion on the CysA-AuNPs and holds disperse, representing a pink color. CysA-AuNPs can be accumulated in the presence of cysteine. The reason is attributed to the interactions of the cysteine-cysteine molecules through thiol interactions. CysA-AuNPs can interact with cysteine-cysteine complexes through amino groups of the CysA on the surface of AuNPs, and carboxylic acid groups of cysteine, leading to agglomeration of CysA-AuNPs. CysA-AuNPs aggregation in the presence of cysteine can alter the color of the probe solution from pink to royal purple, which changed to ruby pink in the presence of Hg2+, to ruby red in the presence of Cu2+, or to blue in the presence of the other ions, which can be detected by a naked eye. Thus, by evaluating the combinatorial colorimetric reactions of each sensing element, the discrimination of all of the tested ions was possible. Schematic 1 represents several interactions between amino acids and AuNPs-capped cysteamine through the colorimetric assay. We supposed that some of the applied amino acids linked to the CysA-AuNPs surface and some of them are free in the probe solution, based on this, we suggested the following mechanisms (Scheme 1). Firstly, Scheme 1A represents no interaction with CysA-AuNPs, and consequently, no aggregation has occurred. It is noteworthy that in this procedure, there may be interplays between metal ions and amino acids, but CysA-AuNPs well-dispersed through the solution in the both cases. Secondly, (Scheme 2 B) indicated that metal ions can stimulate the CysA-AuNPs aggregation and leads to a color change. In this case, amino acids show no effect on the colorimetric answers. Thirdly, according to (Scheme 1C), amino acids can react with the applied metal ions that typically aggregate AuNPs and avoid the aggregation. In the last scenario, Schematic 1D, amino acids and metal ions can have co-contribution effects on CysA-AuNPs aggregation. Moreover, the amino acid-related mechanisms stimulated CysA-AuNPs aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice of optimum type of additive for colorimetric identification of ions
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned before, the mercury and copper ions are extremely ubiquitous environmental pollutants which are dangerous for human health. Thus, there is a necessity for a sensitive and simple method for detection of these ions in aqueous solutions.In this research work, l-aspartic acid and several amino acids were studied to evaluate the colorimetric sensing of the mercury and copper ions, containing l-glycine, l-Leucine, l-alanine, l-arginine, l-valine, l-cysteine, and l-methionine. Some types of amino acids along with their specific features are reviewed in Fig. S1 (see supporting information). The thiol group-consisting amino acids such as cysteine can selectively recognize Hg2+ from the other ions, than other existing amino acids with no structural thiol group such as Leucine, arginine, alanine, methionine, valine, and glycine, as well as aspartic acid. Thiol group-comprising amino acids such as cysteine can sense Hg2+, because of the binding of thiol group of cysteine to the Hg2+ bound probe and form the stable complex of Hg (Cys)2 [40]. The cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs were used as a detection probe for Hg2+ in the presence of cysteine. As mentioned before, cysteamine could be attached to the AuNPs through the thiol groups by Au–S bonds. The CysA–AuNPs showed a stable dispersion through the solution respect to the electrostatic repulsion of the cysteamine on gold nanoparticles, representing a pink color. In the absence of ions, the amine groups of cysteamine can interact with the carboxylic acid groups of cysteine (1 mM), which is accompanied by color alternation from pink to royal purple. These interactions might disintegrate in the presence of Hg2+ ion due to high thiophilicity of the cysteine molecules. Thus, Hg2+-cysteine interaction dominate the NH2 – COOH- interactions of cysteamine-cysteine, leading to deagglomeration of CysA-AuNPs, that is accompanied by color alternation from royal purple to ruby pink. However, deagglomeration of CysA-AuNPs are not completely performed in the presence of Hg2+ and cysteine amino acid molecules, and some of the particles have still remained accumulated. The reason can be attributed to the interactions between Hg2+ and the amine groups of the CysA-AuNPs, thus, the amino groups of cysteamine started to be deprotonated and form N–Hg2+–N structure to stimulate the aggregation of CysA-AuNPs, in which the color alternation to ruby pink was occurred by the naked eye. In the presence of other ions, except Hg2+, ions show no efficient and critical role on CysA-AuNPs deagglomeration, but support CysA-AuNPs accumulation through ion-amine group's interactions, in the presence of all applied amino acids that listed above. However, the scenario is different in the presence of cysteine (10 mM). The higher concentration of cysteine caused to detach some of the thiol binds of cysteamine from AuNPs, and form new binds between cysteiene and cysteamine molecules. The intact AuNPs-CysA are linked with the cysteine-cysteamine complexes through electrostatic interactions between the carboxyl groups of cysteine-cysteamine complexes, and the amine groups of AuNP-CysA, while the free AuNPs are dispersed throughout the solution by assembling aroung the negatively charged amine groups. In the presence of Hg2+, the dispersed AuNPs might be agglomerated and sediment, because of the formation of the thiol-ion, and NH-ion binds. In addition, since methionine and cysteine are chelator for Cu2+, the presence of these amino acids caused to chelate this ion through amine/ carboxylic acid groups, and thiol/amine groups for methionine, and cysteine rings, respectively. Thus, AuNPs-CysA were deagglomerated and amino acid-Cu2+ chelating rings are sediment after some minutes. These phenomena are accompanied by color alternation of the solution from light pink to ruby red in the presence of Cu2+.

To further confirm the deprotonation and protonation of the cysteamine terminal amine group, the zeta potential, which associates with the local environment and the surface charge of particles, was examined. The zeta potential measurement is a useful approach with the ability to offer data on the net charge of nanoparticles and has an overview onto their stability. It is described that the zeta potential can further be applied to deduce the stability of the particle with 30 mV value, in which more than 30 mV values dedicate stability. At pH 7.4, the zeta potential measurement represents a negative value for AuNP-CysA (-25 mV), denoted the deprotonation on the surface of AuNP-CysA, and positive values (13.7 mV) were observed after interacting with Hg2+. Shifting of negative charge to positive charge on AuNP-CysA exhibited that the mercury ion stimulated the tendency towards aggregation. In agreement with the zeta potential results, the size of the CysA-AuNPs are significantly increased after Hg2+ addition, from 15.38 nm to 1054 nm (Fig. S2 (see supporting information)). Hg2+ has tendency to interact with cysteine via thiol-ion interactions, which consequently neutralize the effect of cysteine for AuNPs agglomeration through binding the thiol group of cysteine to AuNPs. However, since the concentration of cysteine is more than Hg2+ ion, some of the cysteine molecules still remained to interact with CysA-AuNPs, thus, the size of the AuNPs was raised after adding cysteine, and Hg2+ ion.

In fact, in the presence of cysteine amino acid (1 mM), the free amine groups of AuNP-CysA might be interacted to the free carboxyl groups of cysteine through electrostatic interactions. On the other side, the free thiol groups of two cysteine-interacted AuNP-CysA might interacted to each other via thiol-thiol interplays. Therefore, the total electron density of the system is related to the free amine groups of the cysteine (Scheme 2A). In the presence of Hg2+, the thiol-thiol binds between cysteine-interacted AuNP-CysA might be ruined to form ionic interactions. Moreover, the electrostatic interactions between amine groups of AuNP-CysA and carboxyl groups of cysteine might be ruined to form NHHg2+ NH binds, triggering two amine groups of AuNP-CysA particles. Moreover, the detached cysteine molecules are interacted with S-Hg2+-S interaction, from one side, and NHHg2+ NH interaction from the other side (Scheme 2C). Thus, AuNP-CysA nanoparticles are partially accumulated in the presence of Hg2+, which is in accordance with the DLS results, where the size of the AuNP-CysA particles increased from 15.38 nm to 1054 nm. Moreover, the total electron density of the system, which was dependent on the free amine groups of cysteine-interacted AuNP-CysA molecules, decreased from −25mv to 13.7 mV in the presence of Hg2+, regarding to the previously described NHHg2+ NH interactions between two AuNPs-CysA molecules. Despite low concentrations of cysteine (1 mM) cannot ruin the thiol-gold binds of AuNPs-CysA molecules, more concentrations of cysteine (10 mM) can detached the cysteamine molecules from AuNPs. In this case, some of the cysteamine molecules are detached from AuNPs, and form new binds with the high concentrated cysteine through thiol-thiol binds. The amounts of the detached cysteamine molecules are depended to the concentration of cysteine molecules. Therefore, the defunctionalized AuNPs are assembled throughout the solution under the effects of free carboxyl and amine groups (Scheme 3 A). Regarding to this fact, the size of the AuNP-CusA after AuNPs defunctionalization in the presence of highly concentrated cysteine (10 mM) is approximately similar to that for the bare AuNPs-CysA molecules (15.38 nm) (Fig. S3 (see supporting information). Previously, researchers introduced cysteine and methionine as chelator for Cu2+ [62], hence, in this project we used AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM), and AuNP-CysA supported methionine (10 mM) for colorimetric detection of Cu2+. The addition of Cu2+ to the AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine solution caused to detach the thiol-thiol binds between the free cysteine-cysteamine complexes, and participate cysteine molecules in the chelating process of Cu2+. Thus, the chelating ring of cysteine is in contact with Cu2+ ion by its thiol and OH groups, while the NH– group is free in the edges of the ring. AuNP-CysA can be assembled around the NH– groups of the chelating ring (scheme 3C). Therefore, the average size of the particles in the system were incredibly increased, reaching from 19.18 nm (for AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM)) to 2210 nm (for AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) in the presence of Cu2+). Moreover, since the total electron density of the system was related to the free electron-rich functional groups (amine and carboxyl groups of cysteamine and cysteine), as illustrated in Scheme 3C, with respect to the chelating procedure of Cu2+, no free electron-rich groups are remained in the system in the presence of this ion. Thus, the total charge of the system changed from −3.18 (for AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM)) to 22.1 (for AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) in the presence of Cu2+) (Fig. S4 (see supporting information)). When methionine was mixed with the AuNPs-CysA solution, the carboxyl group of methionine was interacted with the amine group of cysteamine through electrostatic interactions (Scheme 3D). Thus, the size of the AuNPs-CysA supported methionine raised to 88.43 nm, which is considerably lower than the size of the AuNP-CysA particles in the presence of cysteine. The reason is attributed to the cysteamine molecules detachment from some of the AuNPs in the presence of cysteine to form cysteine-cysteamine complexes via thiol-thiol binds, leading to slight size increment. Using methionine as the supported additive, the electron-rich amine groups of the AuNP-CysA particles are in electrostatic interactions with the electron-rich carboxyl groups of methionine, therefore, the total electron density of the system was decreased from −25 mV (for AuNPs-CysA) to −3.18 mV (for AuNPs-CysA supported methionine) (Fig. S5 (see supporting information)). Despite this decrement in the total electron density of the system in the presence of methionine, the charge of the system is still negative because of the free amine group and CH3 group of methionine. As previously mentioned, methionine amino acid has considered as another chelating agent for Cu2+. The chelating procedure of Cu2+ by methionine is schematically discussed in Scheme 3E. During the chelating process, Cu2+ is interacted with the OH and amine groups of the chelator ring (methionine 10 mM), and the S- groups are remained free on the edges of the chelating ring, which can interact with the amine groups of AuNP-CysA through electrostatic interactions (Scheme 3F). Therefore, the chelating process of Cu2+ by methionine caused to raise the size of the particles in the system from 88.43 nm (for AuNP-CysA supported methionine (10 mM)) to 107.6 nm (for AuNP-CysA supported methionine (10 mM) in the presence of Cu2+) (Fig. S6 (see supporting information)). However, the size increment of GQD-DPA supported cysteine in the presence of Cu2+ is considerably lower than that for the GQD-DPA supported cysteine in the presence of Cu2+. The reason is attributed to the NH– groups on the edges of the cysteine chelating ring which can be interacted with the surrounded AuNPs-CysA particles via electrostatic interactions. The electron density of the AuNP-CysA supported methionine (10 mM) is slightly increased in the presence of Cu2+ from −3.83 mV to −10 mV. The reason can be attributed to the destruction of the methionine binds with AuNPs-CysA because of the chelating process of Cu2+ by methionine. Therefore, the presence of the free electron-rich amine groups of cysteamine on the AuNPs-CysA leads to raise the total electron density of the system (Fig. S6).

Figs. S7&S8 (see supporting information) illustrate the TEM, and SEM images of the untreated AuNPs (A), and CysA treated AuNPs (B). The particle dimension of CysA-capped AuNPs was attained in the average range of 23 nm, which, according to the previous studies, is smaller and narrower than the untreated nanoparticles [63,64]. The images indicate the spherical morphology for both treated, and bare AuNPs, yet, less aggregation of nanoparticles is evident for the cysteamine-treated AuNPs, than the untreated group, which is possibly due to the intermolecular repulsion of the CysA-AuNPs that leads to disperse them in the solution. In fact, cysteamine molecules interact with AuNPs from their thiol portion, making gold-thiol interaction, and their free amine groups with electron-rich nitrogen are remained on the surface of the CysA-AuNPs complex. These negative charges cause to electrostatically repulsion between the particles and make a well-dispersed CysA-AuNPs solution as evident in the TEM and SEM images (Figs. S7&S8 (see supporting information)). Regarding to this fact, the stability of the solution is raised and the color of the solution changed from wine red to pink referring the particle size alternation.

According to the EDS analysis of cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs (Fig. S9 (see supporting information)), signals for carbon (C), oxygen (O), sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) elements are appeared. N, and S elements confirm the presence of amine group, and thiol group, respectively, in the conformational structure of both cysteine, and cysteamine. Further, O and C elements confirm the presence of COO– in the structure of cysteine, while the presence of Au belongs to AuNPs.

The FTIR spectra of CysA-AuNPs are illustrated in Fig. S10 (see supporting information). The main broad band is appeared at around 3687 cm−1 reveals the presence of hydroxyl groups of hydroxides from the synthesized materials. Though, the intense, small peak ~1178 cm−1 is related to the amine group bending of cysteamine. The peak at 1460 cm−1 is attributed to the existence of the C─N bond in cysteamine. The obvious peaks at 1604 cm−1 is attributed to AuNPs. The major peaks of the CysA-AuNPs are occurred at three peaks of 2345 cm−1, 2847 cm−1 and 2924 cm−1, relating to CH, and CH2 groups of cysteine. Furthermore, the NH is appeared at 3796 cm−1 which are specific peak of amide bonds. The peak related to the -SH group at 2558 cm−1, the thiol group characteristic peak of cysteamine, vanished after functionalization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-aggregation sensing platforms are powerful methods to detect mercury ion, for both biological, and environmental usages [65,66]. The electrostatic repulsion of the cysteamine as the positive capping factors against AuNPs aggregation leads to preserve the stability of the cysteamine-treated Au NPs in the aqueous solution. The aggregation of AuNPs could also be triggered by cysteine molecules because of their amino groups with electron-rich nitrogen atoms (Scheme 2A). For example, researchers revealed that 2-mercaptobenzothiazole could stimulate AuNPs aggregation by creating cross-linkages through gold-thiol bonds. While the existence of Hg2+ ions, the chelation interplays between Hg2+ ions, and 2-mercaptobenzothiazole will hinder its capability to stimulate the AuNPs aggregation [67,68]. In our work, as illustrated in Scheme 2, amino acids, cysteine in particular, could be interacted to the CysA-AuNPs via electrostatic interactions between the amine groups of CysA, and the carboxylic acid group of the amino acids, with a corresponding color alternation (light pink to royal purple) in its bare condition. The main concept of the Hg2+ colorimetric sensing is schemed in Scheme 2.

As illustrated in Scheme 2A, low concentrations of cysteine has not any destructive effects on the thiol-AuNP bind and forms weak electrostatic links with the amine groups on AuNPs-CysA particles. When cysteine, which can stimulate an obvious CysA-Au NPs aggregation, is treated with mercury ion (with standard concentration of 1 μg/ml) the color of the gold containing solution is changed from royal purple to ruby pink, which is related to the alternation in the aggregation state of AuNPs. In fact, owing to the high thiophilicity of Hg2+, the presence of this ion caused to thiol-AuNPs binds detachment, and thiol-ion formation that is followed by the anti-aggregation of AuNPs-CysA. However, some of the AuNPs-CysA are still gathered to each other through NHHg2+ NH binds (scheme 2C).

In the absence of mercury ion, the presence of cysteine (1 mM) in the Au NPs-CysA solution meaningfully stimulates a red shift of maximal absorption band of CysA-AuNPs, and the color of the solution changes to royal purple, respecting to the cysteine-induced aggregation. Though, in exposure to Hg2+ (1 μg/ml), the maximal absorption band associated with AuNPs goes through a reverse procedure, and the color rechange to ruby pink (Fig. 4 ), indicating that Hg2+ significantly prevents cysteine from stimulating the Au NPs aggregation. As mentioned before, the chief reason for mercury ion- cysteine interaction is the high thiophilicity of cysteine which causes to interplay with Hg2+, so that, no/little chance is leaved for cysteine to interact with the AuNPs. Thus, Hg2+ leads to deactivate the aggregation role of cysteine. Hence, Hg2+ could be detected through color change monitoring of Au NPs solution via naked eye.

Low concentrations of cysteine (1 mM) cannot break the thiol-AuNPs binds of AuNPs-CysA, thus, the cysteine-cysteine complexes can interact with the amine groups of AuNPs-CysA through electrostatic interactions. This interaction between cysteine and AuNPs-CysA leading to AuNPs-CysA agglomeration that is accompanied by the color alternation of the AuNPs-CysA solution from light pink to royal blue after adding cysteine amino acid (Fig. 4). More concentrations of cysteine (10 mM) can break the thiol-AuNPs binds and form new binds of cysteine-cysteamine through thiol-thiol interactions, causing to AuNPs-CysA deagglomeration. This result can be confirmed by DLS which indicates the destruction of AuNPs-CysA clusters in the presence of high-concentrated cysteine. However, it is obvious that some of the AuNPs-CysA binds are still remained intact, since the de-agglomeration behavior of the AuNPs-CysA solution by cysteine is directly dependent on the concentration of cysteine. Therefore, the color of the AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) was changed from light pink to purple (Fig. 6).

When Hg2+ was added to the AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM), the thiol-AuNPs binds between AuNPs and cysteamine might be interacted under the effect of ionic interactions of Hg2+ with thiol groups. Therefore, the AuNPs-CysA might be deagglomerated and the new binds of S-Hg2+-S, and NH-Hg2+–NH might be formed with Hg2+-cysteine, and Hg2+- AuNPs-CysA molecules, respectively. This deagglomeration is accompanied by color alternation of the solution from light pink to light ruby pink (Fig. 4). However, the chemical procedure is totally different for AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM). In this case, as mentioned above, since some of the AuNPs lose their binds with cysteamine in the presence of cysteine (10 mM), the presence of Hg2+ leads to cysteine-cysteamine detachment. Thus, the cysteine molecule is in contact with Hg2+ from the terminal thiol group of one side, and the terminal amine group from the other side. Therefore, the free AuNPs which were assembled throughout the solution under the effects of the terminal amine and carboxyl groups of the cysteine-cysteamine complexes, might make AuNPs clusters in the presence of Hg2+ and Cu2+, owing to the electrostatic repulsions with the positively charged ion. In fact, as previously mentioned, when high concentrated cysteine (10 mM) was added to the AuNP-CysA system, some of the cysteamine molecules might lose their binds to participate in the thiol-thiol interactions of cysteine-cysteamine. The rate of cysteamine-AuNPs detachment by cysteine is directly depended on the concentration of cysteine. Therefore, in the absence of ions, the AuNPs are dispersed throughout the system under the effects of the electrostatic interactions with the electron-rich amine groups on the edges of the cysteine-cysteamine complexes. Moreover, the remained AuNP-CysA are interacted with cysteine-cysteamine complexes through the weak hydrogen/electrostatic interactions between the amine groups of AuNP-CysA, and the carboxyl groups of cysteine in the cysteine-cysteamine complexes. When Hg2+ and Cu2+ were introduced to the AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) system, the thiol-thiol binds between cysteine and cysteamine were detached and cysteine molecules might be participated in the chelating interactions with Cu2+, and the thiophilic interactions with Hg2+. Moreover, there is the possibility for cysteamine molecules to lose their binds with AuNPs to contribute in the thiophilic interactions with Hg2+. Therefore, not only the AuNPs can be significantly clustered, and sediment in the presence of both Cu2+, and Hg2+. These clusters can be appeared in the form of pink sediments in the AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 5
).

Regardless of the concentration of cysteine, the chemical procedure of ion detection by AuNP-CysA supported cysteine solution is different in the presence of Cu2+, respecting to the chelating response of cysteine towards Cu2+. When Cu2+ was added to the system, the cysteine molecules were ruined their binds with other cysteine/cysteamine molecules (in higher concentrations of cysteine (10 mM)), or with AuNP-CysA (in lower concentrations of cysteine (1 mM) to participate in the chelating procedure of Cu2+. Cu2+ can be contributed in the chelating process through binding with OH, and SH groups of cysteine. The chelating process of Cu2+ is accompanied by AuNPs-CysA deagglomeration, and consequently, color alternation of the AuNPs-CysA solution from light pink to ruby red (Fig. 6). The final color of the solution is not depended to the cysteine concentration, because the similar process of deagglomeration were occurred in both cases.

The process of ion detection was also performed in the AuNPs-CysA supported methionine (10 mM). Methionine is a thiol-contained amino acid which is in contact with the AuNPs-CysA molecules through electrostatic interactions between the carboxyl group of methionine and the amine group of AuNPs-CysA. Therefore, AuNPs are not clustered in the presence of methionine, and no color alternation was occurred. This fact is confirmed by the DLS results which shows slight increment in the size of the AuNPs-CysA particles, owing to the above mentioned electrostatic interactions between methionine and AuNPs-CysA. As previously mentioned, methionine is another chelator for Cu2+. In the presence of Cu2+, amine groups and carboxyl groups of methionine are participated in the chelating ring formation around Cu2+, therefore, the size of the AuNPs-cysteine might be reduced because the methionine molecules lost their binds with AuNP-CysA. Thus, the chelating procedure of Cu2+ in AuNP-CysA supported methionine is accompanied by color alternation of the solution from light pink to sky blue. Other ions caused to accumulate the AuNPs-CysA supported methionine molecules through NH–-ionn+, NH– interactions with the amine group of methionine/cysteamine, leading to color alternation of the sensing solution from light pink to blue (Fig. 7 ).

As previously mentioned, the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs might be deagglomerated in the presence of Hg2+ ions, specifically. In fact, Hg2+ might interact with cysteine and defeat the amine–amine interactions and amine-carboxylic acid interactions between CysA-AuNPs-cysteine, thanks to the high thiophilicity of cysteine. Thus, some of the cysteine molecules dettached from CysA-AuNPs, leading to leave the electron-rich nitrogen groups on the AuNPs-CysA. However, Fig. S11B (see supporting information) reveals positive charge for the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs complex in the presence of Hg2+. With this regard, it can be assumed that the facial amine groups of CysA-AuNPs might be deprotonated in the presence of Hg2+ leading to make N-Hg2+-N interactions, which can accumulate the CysA-AuNPs, partially. Therefore, the electrical charge of the system changed from −25 mV to + 13.7 mV as illustrated in zeta potential results. Furthermore, the comparison between the particle size of CysA-AuNPs alone or in the presence of cysteine and Hg2+ ion showed significant increment in the size of the CysA-AuNPs in the presence of Hg2+ from 97.8 nm to 1054 nm (Z-average), which is referring to the accumulation of CysA-AuNPs in the presence of Hg2+, as lately discussed. As previously mentioned, TEM image reveals spherical-shaped AuNPs, and CysA-AuNPs, in which the particle dimensions of AuNPs are decreased after capping with cysteamine, due to particle dispersity. The CysA-AuNPs dispersity is due to the interparticle repulsion arisen from their negative charges. In confirmation with the TEM and SEM results, the AFM results reveal a decrement in the size of the CysA-AuNPs because of the disaggregation of the AuNPs as the influence of cysteamine. As previously mentioned, the reason of this disaggregation in the AuNPs is attributed to the negative charge of the CysA-AuNPs (-25 mV according to the zeta potential results). In fact, while the thiol groups of the cysteamine molecules are interacted with AuNPs, the electron-rich amine groups are remained free on the surface of CysA-AuNPs, providing negatively-charged particles where interparticle repulsion caused to their deagglomeration and decrease the particle size of CysA-AuNPs. Therefore, the AFM images show the particle sizes of the CysA-AuNPs (33 nm in average) are considerably lower than the particle size of AuNPs (13 nm in average). Moreover, the AFM images indicate a considerable increment in the particle size of CysA-AuNPs after adding Hg2+, in the presence of cysteine, from 33 nm to 106 nm. This size increment is attributed to the interactions between Hg2+, and free facial amine groups on the structural plan of CysA-AuNPs. In fact, firstly, these free amine groups were participated in amine–amine, and amine-carboxylic acid group's interplays of cysteamine-cysteine, leading to CysA-AuNPs agglomeration. After adding Hg2+, since the high thiophilicity of the cysteine molecules, Hg2+-thiol interactions defeat the amine–amine, and amine-carboxylic acid groups interplays of cysteamine-cysteine, causing to CysA-AuNPs deagglomeration. Therefore, the possibility might be provided for Hg2+ to deprotonate the nitrogen of the facial amine groups on the CysA-AuNPs, and form N-Hg2+-N binds, causing to partially accumulation of these particles, and raising the particle size to 106 nm, as mentioned in the AFM results. These results are in confirmation with the colorimetric detection of Hg (II), which indicates the color alternation of the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs solution after adding Hg2+ from royal purple to reby pink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity recognition of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by CysA-AuNPs supported cysteine in the presence of various ions
ce:section.ce:para: It should be noted that amino acids were rarely been applied to identify metal ions, since it has been stated that these materials were not selective for an especial metal ion. To examine the selectivity of this sensing device towards Hg2+ and Cu2+ detection, several metal ions containing Hg2+ and Cu2+ were added to the CysA-AuNPs solution, incorporating with aspartic acid and various amino acids among arginine, alanine, cysteine, glycine, leucine, methionine, and valine, as additives (all concentrations are 1 mM). The same concentrations (1 ppm standard solution) of divalent metal ions Hg2+, As3+, Sr2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Te4+, Zr4+, K+, and Li+ were investigated in the presence of the mentioned amino acids-CysA-AuNP solutions, separately. According to Fig. 9, it is obvious that the color of the sensing solution changed to blue in the presence of all the tested ions, except for Hg2+, which changes the color of cysteine (1 mM)-treated CysA-AuNPs from royal purple to ruby pink. As previously mentioned, in the presence of Hg2+, the cysteine molecules in the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs complex possess more tendency to break the amine-carboxylic acid binds between AuNPs-CysA and cysteine-cysteine complexes and interact with Hg2+ through Hg2+-thiol binds, owing to high thiophilicity of the cysteine amino acid. Therefore, the presence of Hg2+ caused to deagglomeration of the CysA-AuNPs. However, this deagglomeration was not totally occurred because of the further interactions between the Hg2+ ion molecules and the free amine groups on the CysA-AuNPs. In fact, after cysteine detachment from the CysA-AuNPs, the amine groups of cysteamine remained free on the CysA-AuNPs surface, leading to Hg2+ interaction with the electron-rich nitrogen of the facial amine groups on CysA-AuNPs, through deprotonation of the nitrogen. Thus, a partial aggregation was occurred in CysA-AuNPs, leading to red shift of the UV–vis spectra of CysA-AuNPs, as well as color alternation. Except cysteine, all other amino acids, with no thiol groups on their chain sides, interact with CysA-AuNPs through their amine groups, and carboxylic acid groups with the amine groups of the CysA-AuNPs, leading to CysA-AuNPs agglomeration. On the other side, in the presence of other metal ions, except Hg2+, the interactions between the positively-charged ions and the negatively charged amino acid-supported CysA-AuNPs, causing to more CysA-AuNPs aggregation, and consequently, a visible color change, and UV–vis spectra red shift. Since the agglomeration rate of CysA-AuNPs in the presence of positively charged metal ions (except Hg2+) is assumed to be far more than that in the presence of Hg2+, the presence of the mercury ion can be selected amongst all the other ions through colorimetric sensing, as well as UV–vis spectra shifting.

The alternations in the SPR band of the cysteine- supported CysA-AuNPs with metal ions addition were investigated. As shown in, the absorption peak of CysA-AuNPs is shifted from 520 nm to 640 nm, when Hg2+ is adding to the solution, and the peak was considerably broadened. In the presence of Hg2+, the absorbance at 640 nm is much greater than that in the presence of other ions. Notably, this exceptional selectivity can be detected by the naked eye. These results specify that only Hg2+ ion can considerably raise the catalytic activity of CysA-AuNPs nanocomposite in the presence of cysteine, among the other metal ions. The colorimetric results of the sensing behaviors of a wide variety of ions in the presence of cysteine-treated CysA-AuNPs are illustrated in Fig. 9. It is indicated that just Hg2+ could understand the anti-aggregation of cysteine supported-CysA-AuNPs and shows an excellent selectivity toward Hg2+ detection over the other investigated metal ions. These reporting data are in agreement with the UV–vis spectrum results of various ions in exposure to the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs sensing solution, which demonstrating higher absorption intensity for the mercury ion than that for the other ions, revealing brilliant selectivity for Hg2+ sensing that can be applied for the recognition of actual samples, representing that the offered sensing platform shows a practical application value for Hg2+ detection (Fig. 9). According to the colorimetric results of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine, while the color of the AuNP-CysA solution is altered from pink to royal purple in the presence of cysteine (1 Mm), the other amino acids have not shown any astounding effects on the color alternation of the sensing solution. The reason might be attributed to the thiol group of cysteine, which provides the possibility to bind with the thiol groups of another cysteine molecule, to form new binds of cysteine-cysteine via the interactions between their terminal thiol groups. Therefore, there is the possibility for AuNPs-CysA agglomeration in the presence of cysteine by making the electrostatic interactions between the amine groups of the AuNPs-CysA and the terminal carboxyl groups on the edges of the cysteine-cysteine complexes. The case is not the same for other amino acids in the presence of AuNPs-CysA. In the presence of other amino acids, as well as aspartic acid, the amine group of AuNP-CysA make electrostatic interactions with the carboxyl group of the amino acid, but no AuNPs agglomeration was occurred. Therefore, apart from cysteine, the color of the AuNP-CysA solution might not change in the presence of other amino acids (Fig. 9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Visible spectrophotometry detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Aggregation of AuNPs with proper sizes causes to SPR coupling, and color alternations in the visible region [69]. SPR happens when the vibrational frequency arisen from free electrons interplays with the incident light frequency to produce resonance coupling. This leads to a sharp characteristic absorption for AuNPs in the UV–vis region. The wavelength of the maximum absorption shifts with alternations in the interparticle distances, shapes, and sizes of the AuNPs. For instance, researchers revealed that 13-nm AuNPs show a maximum absorption wavelength at around 523 nm, which appears in wine red color. An increment in the particle dimension or decrement in the interparticle spaces leads to a color change, and a red shift in the maximum absorption wavelength [60,69]. In this study, AuNP-CysA with 15.38 nm in size showed a maximum absorption wavelength at around 535 nm (Fig. S13 (see supporting information). Fig. S13 (see SI) reveals the UV–Vis spectra of colloidal solutions of CysA-AuNPs and cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs. According to Fig. S13A (see supporting information), along with the previous studies [70-72], the intensity of the UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs is considerably diminished after capping with cysteine. This significant shift is attributed to the differences between the interparticle distances and particle sizes of AuNPs, and CysA-AuNPs. The matter of the fact is that, as previously mentioned, since the cysteamine molecules are interacted with AuNPs via thiol-Au interactions, the negatively –charged amine groups are maintained non-bonded on the surface of the CysA-AuNPs, leading to interparticle repulsion arisen from the electrostatic repulsion of the negative charges of the CysA-AuNPs. Thus, the interparticle distances of the CysA-AuNPs might be more, than that for AuNPs. Moreover, as mentioned in AFM images, the average particle size decreased from 13 µm for AuNPs, to 33 nm for CysA-AuNPs, causing to a substantial red shift in their UV–vis peak.

Confirming our results, researchers [73,74] reported that the sulfate in cysteamine is the chief cause for hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction between AuNPs and cysteamine and also triggers the de-aggregation of AuNPs, which is responsible for the shifting of the absorption band of CysA-AuNPs, and subsequently, a color alternation. In the presence of cysteine amino acid, as additive, the intensity of the UV–vis spectrum slightly decreased (Fig. S16 (see supporting information)). As previously mentioned, in the presence of cysteine (1 mM), AuNP-CysA might be slightly aggregated because of the electrostatic interactions between the cysteine amino acid and the amine groups of the AuNPs-CysA. With this slight accumulation, the UV–vis spectra of AuNP-CysA is slightly decreased when supporting with cysteine (1 mM) (Fig. 10). In fact, the energy gap of AuNP-CysA might be decreased after interacting with cysteine, causing to reduce the peak intensity of AuNPs-CysA. Apart from that, in the presence of high concentrated cysteine, more cysteine molecules are attached to the AuNPs-CysA, than in the presence of low-concentrated cysteine. Thus, the energy bind of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) is wider than that for the AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM), which is the chief reason for the peak intensity increment in the first group. This shifting is also attributed to the changes in interparticle distances, and particle sizes of CysA-AuNPs after adding cysteine. When cysteine is added, the facial free amino groups of CysA-AuNPs might interact with the amino groups and carboxylic acid groups of cysteine, leading to CysA-AuNPs accumulation. Thus, the interparticle of CysA-AuNPs might decrease and their size might increase in the presence of cysteine. Therefore a slight shifting in red shift of the UV–vis spectrum of CysA-AuNPs is occurred. In the presence of Hg2+, owing to high thiophilicity of cysteine, this amino acid reacts with the mercury ion, leading to deagglomeration of CysA-AuNPs. On the other side, after cysteine detaching from CysA-AuNPs, Hg2+ molecules find the chance to interact with the free electron-rich nitrogen of the facial amine groups of CysA-AuNPs. Thus the nitrogen might deprotonated and the N-Hg2+-N interactions might occur, leading to partial agglomeration of CysA-AuNPs. Thus, the UV–vis spectra of CysA-AuNPs shifted from 527 nm to 550 nm after interacting with 225 μl of Hg2+ (1 ppm) and the absorption intensity shifted from 0.79 (in the presence of cysteine and absence of Hg2+ ion) to 0.37 (after interacting with Hg2+ ion) as shown in S10 (see supporting information). This phenomenon is accompanied by color alternation (from royal purple to ruby pink) that is visible by naked eye. In this case, the degree of aggregation was related to the concentration of Hg2+, letting their quantification in solution through their UV–vis spectra intensity, which is discussed in the latter sections. The AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM) clusters might be ruined in the presence of Hg2+, because the high thiophilicity of Hg2+ causes to make Hg2+-cysteine complex and ruined the electrostatic binds between AuNPs-CysA and cysteine molecules. Therefore, the deagglomeration of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM) clusters in the presence of Hg2+ leads to raise the peak intensity of UV–vis spectra of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM). In fact, in the presence of Hg2+, since some of the cysteine molecules, which caused to reduce the energy gap of AuNP-CysA, are detached from the AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM) complex, the energy bind of the AuNP-CysA molecules might again increase, which is the main reason for the increment in the peak intensity of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM) after interacting with Hg2+. The deagglomeration rate of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine by Hg2+ is dependent on the concentration of cysteine. The reason is related to the different agglomeration procedures of AuNPs-CysA in the presence of high (10 mM) and low (1 mM) concentrations of cysteine. Unlike the low concentration (1 mM), the presence of high concentrated cysteine (10 mM) caused to break the binds between AuNPs and cysteamine, to form new binds between cysteine and cysteamine through thiol-thiol interactions. Thus, the deagglomeration of AuNP-CysA supported high concentrated cysteine is more than that for AuNP-CysA supported low concentrated cysteine. When Hg2+ was added to the AuNP-CysA supported high concentrated cysteine, the thiol-thiol binds between cysteine and cysteamine might be ruined to form new thiol-ion binds. In this case, Hg2+ interacts with two thiol binds of cysteine/cysteamine molecules, leading to deagglomeration of AuNPs-CysA molecules. On the other hand, the Hg2+-interacted cysteine/cysteamine has a free amine group on its terminal side, which can be interacted with the nearby Hg2+ through NH-Hg2+–NH interaction, triggering an amine group of AuNP-CysA. Therefore, a slight agglomeration might also be occurred in the AuNP-CysA supported cysteine 10 mM in the presence of Hg2+. With the mentioned reasons, the intensity of the Uv–vis peak of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) is slightly decreased in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 12 ).

As previously mentioned, the presence of highly concentrated cysteine caused to detach some of the cysteamine molecules from AuNPs, which is accompanied by the gap energy reduction of AuNP-CysA. But some of the AuNP-CysA molecules are still intact, because the rate of the cysteamine detachment from AuNPs is depended on the concentration of cysteine. When Hg2+ is added to the system, not only cysteine-cysteamine complexes are ruined under the effect of the ionic interactions, but also some of the intact AuNPs-CysA are underwent the ionic influences, leading to cysteamine detachment form AuNPs. With the mentioned reasons, the energy bind of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) might be further reduced in the presence of Hg2+, leading to further peak intensity reduction.

The peak intensity of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) is also raised in the presence of Cu2+. In fact, regardless of the cysteine concentration, this amino acid might acts as a chelator agent for Cu2+ and causes to deagglomerate the AuNPs-CysA clusters. In the presence of Cu2+, a large portion of cysteine molecules lost their electrostatic interactions with the AuNP-CysA molecules and contributed in the chelating procedure of Cu2+. Therefore, the energy bind of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine might be increased in the presence of Cu2+, because of the cysteine removal from the AuNP-CysA complex, leading to raise the intensity of the UV–vis peak of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine might be increased in the presence of Cu2+. However, the intensity of the UV–vis peak of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine in the presence of Cu2+ is still lower than AuNP-CysA, dedicating that some of the cysteine molecules are still remained attached to the AuNP-CysA, and have not been triggered under the effect of chelating interactions. In fact, the amounts of the involved cysteine molecules in the chelating procedure is related to the concentration of the applied Cu2+. More concentrations of the Cu2+ cause to more cysteine molecule detachment form the AuNP-CysA, and consequently, more increment in the width of the energy bind of the AuNP-CysA. Therefore, higher concentrations of Cu2+ shows higher UV–vis peak intensity for AuNP-CysA supported cyetien (10 mM) (Fig. 14). The UV–Vis spectra of different concentrations of Cu2+ in AuNP-CysA supported cysteiene (10 mM) shows that high concentrations of Cu2+ ( ≥ 50 µl) caused to cysteamine separation from AuNPs, because of the UV–vis peak intensity of AuNP-CysA supported cyetien (10 mM) mixed with high concentrations of Cu2+, is more than that for AuNP-CysA.
