This fact means that high concentrations of Cu2+ ( ≥ 50 µl) cause to raise the width of the energy bind of the AuNP-CysA by removing cysteamine from the AuNP-CysA complex. Despite the deagglomeration behavior of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) by Cu2+, when the volume of this Cu2+ (1 µg/ml) exceeds a certain level (15 µl), the UV–vis peak intensity of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) is decreased. In fact, all of the cysteine molecules are detached from the AuNP-CysA complexes in the certain volume of Cu2+ (
≤ 15
µl). Higher volumes of Cu2+ caused to AuNP-CysA agglomeration, owing to the electrostatic interactions between the positively charged Cu2+ molecules, and the negatively charged amine groups of AuNPs-CysA (Fig. 13
). The fact that the peak intensity of AuNP-CysA is reduced after adding cysteine (10 mM) revealed the reduction of the energy bind of AuNP-CysA by cysteine. Since Cu2+ caused to detach the cysteine molecules from AuNPs-CysA, the energy bind of the AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) increased in the presence of Cu2+ (15 µl of Cu2+ (1 µg/ml), 50 µl cysteine (10 mM), and 100 µl of AuNPs-CysA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectrophotometry detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescence spectrometry assay was performed for detection of mercury ion in water, and human urine, as environmental, and biological samples, respectively, based on a “turn-on” fluorescence quenching method induced by mercury ion. According to Fig. 15 , the fluorescence emission of AuNPs approximately overlapped with the fluorescence emission of CysA-AuNPs. The electrostatic repulsion between the cysteamine and AuNPs, which might have negative charges regarding to the synthesis technique of citrate reduction, leading to prevent the fluorescence quenching. Regarding to this fact, the fluorescence spectrum of AuNPs overlapped with that for the CysA-AuNPs (Fig. 15). Therefore, no changes were occurred in the energy bind of AuNP after interacting with cysteamine. With the same reason, pertaining the negatively-charged amino groups and carboxylic acid groups on the cysteine structure, the fluorescence spectrum of CysA-AuNPs also overlapped with the fluorescence spectrum of CysA-AuNPs in the presence of cysteine, despite the fact of CysA-AuNPs aggregation in the presence of cysteine. The matter of the fact is that the inevitable aggregation of CysA-AuNPs in the presence of cysteine is depending on the chemical interactions between the amine groups of cysteamine, and the carboxylic acid groups of cysteine, leading to aggregate the CysA-AuNPs. After adding the positively charged mercury ion, as illustrated in schematic 2-C, Hg2+ molecules interact with the cysteamine molecule, owing to not only the interparticle attraction, but also high thiophilicity of the cysteaine molecules. This interaction causes to trigger the negatively-charged cysteine molecules with the positively-charged mercury ion, leading to defeat the COOH– NH2 – interactions between the amine groups of AuNP-CysA and the carboxyl groups of cysteine-cysteine complexes, leading to deagglomerate the CysA-AuNPs. Regarding to this deagglomeration of CysA-AuNPs after adding Hg2+, the relevant fluorometry peak intensity of CysA-AuNPs-cysteine incredibly raises after adding Hg2+ (Fig. 16 ). In fact, as previously mentioned, cysteine caused to reduce the bind energy of AuNP-CysA, hence, the width of this bind energy might again raise after cysteine molecules detachments from AuNP-CysA by Hg2+, resulting to raise the fluorometry peak intensity of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine accompanied with Hg2+. However, in accordance with the DLS and zeta potential results which respectively represents the size increment and charge alternation (from negative to positive) for CysA-AuNPs after treating with Hg2+ in the presence of cysteine, it seemed that the added Hg2+ not only caused to triggered the cysteine molecules, as mentioned above, but also a portion of these positively-charged metal ions interacted with a portion of the negatively charged CysA-AuNPs through electrostatic interactions, leading to a partial agglomeration in CysA-AuNPs. Thus, since a portion of the negatively charged amino groups of the CysA-AuNPs are triggered with the positively-charged mercury ion, the total charge of the system turned from negative to positive, and the size of the CysA-AuNPs was increased after Hg2+ addition. Apart from Hg2+, the presence of the other ions (As2+, Te4+, Ni2+, Na2+, Mg2+ and Co2+) have no significant effects on the cysteine detachment from the AuNPs-CysA. These ions can make electrostatic binds with the free amine groups of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM) and agglomerate the AuNP-CysA (Scheme 2B). In a close look, the compositing of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM) with the ions (except Hg2+, and Cu2+) caused to increase the defects and gaps in the energy bind of AuNP-CysA, leading to quench the intensity of the fluorometry peak of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM).

A comparison between the chemical interaction procedures of scheme 2C, and 1D, reveals that despite the partial aggregation of CysA-AuNPs-cysteine in the presence of Hg2+, the amounts of CysA-AuNPs aggregations are still superior in the absence of Hg2+, and in the presence of the other ions (e.g., As2+, Mg2+, Te4+, Ni2+, Na2+, and Co2+) owing to the double simultaneous effects of ions and cysteine on CysA-AuNPs aggregation (scheme 2B). Moreover, as illustrated in schematic 2A, the rate of CysA-AuNPs aggregation in the presence of cysteine and positively charged metal ions are more than that for CysA-AuNPs in the presence of cysteine, and the absence of the positively charged metal ions. This higher amounts of CysA-AuNPs aggregation in the presence of cysteine and metal ions , except Hg2+, lead to quench the fluorometry spectrum of the CysA-AuNPs in the presence of only cysteine (Fig. S14) (see supporting information). However, for Hg2+, as previously discussed, the aggregation rate is lower than the aggregation rate of CysA-AuNPs in the presence of only cysteine, leading to increase its UV–vis spectrum intensity (Fig. S13 (see supporting information)). With this regard, the intensities of the recorded fluorescence spectrums were inversely proportional to the concentration of the Hg2+ samples, ranging from 0.001 ppm to 1 ppm. In fact, as shown in Schematic 2B, the increment in the Hg2+ concentration causes to raise the possibility of the interplays between the negatively charged CysA-AuNPs, and the positively charged ions, leading to more aggregation of CysA-AuNPs, and consequently, more rate of quenching. Thus, in human urine sample, the Hg2+ concentration follows a direct relationship with the quenching rate of the AuNP-CysA fluorometry peak (Fig. S14 (see supporting information)). On the other coincide, since the increment of Hg2+ concentration causes to more cysteien-Hg2+ interactions, the wavelength relating to the maximum peak of CysA-AuNPs followed an ascending trend, shifting from 421 nm for urine-AuNP-CysA in the presence of cysteine and absence of Hg2+. All in all, the result indicated that the electrostatic interplays between two particles did occur due to the charge distribution on CysA-AuNPs might be altered in the biologic (urine sample) or environmental (water sample) solution medium, which would cause to static fluorescence quenching.

As previously mentioned, high concentrations of cysteine (10 mM) caused to some of the cysteamine detachment from AuNPs, to form new binds of cysteine-cysteamine, while some of the AuNPs-CysA molecules are remained intact in the presence of cysteine (10 mM). Cysteine caused to decrease the width of the energy bind of AuNP-CysA. After introducing Cu2+, cysteine molecules are detached from AuNP-CysA and caused to increment of the width of the energy bind of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine amino acid (10 mM). Therefore, the fluorometry peak intensity of the AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) is raised with Cu2+. In the presence of the other ions, the remained AuNPs-CysA might be agglomerated with the positively charged ions under the electrostatic effects of the negatively charged amine groups on AuNPs-CysA, leading to quench the fluorometry peak of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) (Fig. 16).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the optical probe for colorimetric detection of Hg2+ in the presence of other ions
ce:section.ce:para: A simple colorimetric test based on Au NPs anti-aggregation is suggested for the monitoring of Hg2+. To assess the sensitivity of the sensing system, different concentrations of 0.001 ppm–1 ppm of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions were tested. According to the previous studies [19,61], L-cysteine-modified AuNPs was considered as the sensing probe for Hg2+ detection. However, their proposed probe did not show immediate color alternation in the presence of Hg2+. But, after the UV–vis spectra measurement, the mixture solutions color was changed to purple or blue gray in 5 min. They revealed alternations in the SPR of the cysteine-treated AuNPs after eight times measurements, and when the Hg2+ concentration was in the range of 0.01 to 0.05 μM, the Cysteine-treated AuNPs solution remained a red color [19]. However, in our study, the color change was observed by naked eye, immediately after adding ions to the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs solution, alongside with immediate shifting of the SPR measurements peak of cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs. From Fig. S15 and S16 (see supporting information), when the high concentration of Hg2+ (1 ppm) was added to the AuNP-CysA solution, the intensity of the UV–Vis SPR peak shifted from 0.75 (for CysA-AuNPs) to 0.37 (for cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs after adding Hg2+), and their relevant UV–Vis peak shifted from 527 nm to 550 nm. Furthermore, when the intensity of the mercury ion was raised from 0.001 μg/ml to 1 μg/ml, a gradual decrease in the intensity of SPR at 550 nm was revealed. This suggesting that the CysA-AuNPs aggregation in the presence of Hg2+, and cysteine is dependent on the Hg2+ concentration. Obviously, the results indicated different rates of deagglomeration of CysA-AuNPs, and interparticle distances by increasing Hg2+ concentration. In fact, more concentrations of Hg2+ caused to interact with more amounts of cysteine molecules, and hence, more disaggregation of CysA-AuNPs was occurred.

In the presence of cysteine (1 mM), the AuNPs are agglomerated. This agglomeration process is based on the electrostatic interactions between the amine groups of cysteamine on the edges of AuNPs-CysA, and the carboxyl groups on the cysteine-cysteine complexes. This agglomeration is the main reason for color alternation of the AuNPs-CysA solution in the presence of cysteine (1 mM), from light pink to royal blue. The composition of cysteine (1 mM) with AuNPs-CysA caused to reduce the bind energy of AuNPs-CysA, leading to decrease the UV–vis peak intensity, and quenching the fluorimetry spectra of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM). In the presence of Hg2+, the cysteine molecules might loose their contact with AuNP-CysA to participate in the ionic interactions with Hg2+ through thiol-ion interactions. Therefore, Hg2+ leads to AuNPs-CysA deagglomeration. Besides, the cysteine decomposition from AuNPs-CysA complexes caused to increase the width of the energy bind of AuNPs supported cysteine (1 mM), leading to raise the UV–Vis peak intensity and raise the fluorometry peak intensity of the AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (1 mM). When the concentration of cysteine is raised to 10 mM, the binds between some of the AuNPs and cysteamine molecules might be detached to form cysteine-cysteamine interactions by interacting their terminal thiol groups. The amounts of these binds detachments are depended on the concentration of the added cysteine. In this case, three kinds of particles might be existed in the system among AuNPs, AuNPs-CysA, cysteine-cysteamine complexes. Therefore, the amine groups AuNPs-CysA make electrostatic binds with the carboxyl groups of cysteine-cysteamine complexes, and the AuNPs are disperesed throughout the system by assembling around the negatively charged amine groups of the cysteine-cysteamine complexes. When Hg2+ is added to the system, the interactions between cysteine and cysteamine complexes might be ruined to form S-Hg2+-S binds from one side, and NH-Hg2+–NH binds from the other side. Therefore the AuNPs which were assembled under the influence of the negatively charged amine groups of cysteine-cysteamine complexes might be clustered and sediment.

Thus, both colorimetric alternations and UV–vis spectra alternations were occurred in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+. The color of the cysteine-treated CysA-AuNP solution in the presence of Hg2+ with concentration range between 0.1 μg/ml to 1 μg/ml was changed from royal purple (for cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs) to rouged. This color was re-changed in the presence of Hg2+ concentration ranging from 0.01 μg/ml to 0.04 μg/ml to blue. Moreover, the color of the cysteine-treated CysA-AuNPs solution was re-changed to elephant pink color, in the presence of Hg2+ with 0.001 μg/ml concentration, at the SPR peak point of 540 nm (Fig. S16 (see supporting information). Fig. S16 (SI) shows the camera captured images of Hg2+ in different concentrations, detected by CysA-AuNPs with the support of cysteine, in three times separate repetitions. All three groups showed similar color alternations in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+. These absorbance are related to the coupled plasmon absorbance of AuNPs in close contact. Different SPR peak points of the CysA-AuNPs solution are attributed to the different quantities of CysA-AuNPs aggregation. The UV–vis absorption ratio follows an increasing trend by increasing the concentration of Hg2+, through a linear relationship. Fig. S16 (see supporting information) reveals the plot of ΔI/intensity of CysA-AuNPs in the presence of different concentrations of mercury ion (from 0.01 ppm to 1 ppm) versus the Hg2+ concentrations. The error bars show standard deviations on the base of three separate measurements applied the values of the absorbance to express the aggregated molar ratio of CysA-AuNPs. The immediate color alternations of cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs in exposure to all concentrations of Hg2+ reveal the sensitivity of the system for detecting Hg2+ as low as 0.001 ppm in both water sample, and biological fluids (human urine and plasma samples). Therefore, we propose that the cysteine-treated AuNP-CysA can be applied for Hg2+ detecting with the minimum sensing concentration of 0.001 ppm, observing immediately and by naked eyes, which can be described as the mercury ion is expected to link with the cysteine molecule through cooperative ligand–metal interactions, causing to the disruption of aggregates and interparticle distances alternations. The incorporation of Hg2+ could stimulate the SPR band of cysteine-treated CysA-AuNPs solution transferring to the opposite direction. As previously mentioned, Hg2+ caused to cysteine decomposition from the AuNP-CysA complexes, because of the high thiophilicity of Hg2+, and form new binds with Hg2+ through its terminal thiol group. It is obvious that more concentrations of Hg2+ leads to more cysteine molecules decomposition from AuNPs-CysA complexes. Since the bind energy of AuNPs-CysA was decreased in the presence of cysteine, the removal of this component from the system leads to raise the energy bind of the AuNPs-CysA, therefore, more concentrations of Hg2+ appeared higher UV–Vis, and fluorometry peak intensities.

Moreover, different concentrations of Cu2+ from 0.01 µg/ml to 0.4 µg/ml were considered to reach out the LOD of Cu2+ detection by AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) solution. As illustrated in Fig. 17 , Cu2+ (0.01 µg/ml) in the presence of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine 10 mM changed the color of the sensing solution from pink to blue. The color alternation of the AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) in the presence of very low concentration of Cu2+ (0.01 µg/ml) is similar with the color alternation of the non-thiophilic ions in AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM). This fact revealed that the very low concentrations of Cu2+ cannot stimulated cysteine to make chelating rings, and make electrostatic interactions between the positively charged low concentrated ion, and the negatively charged amine groups of cysteine, leading to partial agglomeration of the AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine (10 mM) solution and color alternation form pink to blue. However, Cu2+ with higher concentrations ( ≥ 0.01 µg/ml) caused to stimulate cysteine to chelate Cu2+, which can be appeared with color alternation of the solution from pink to ruby red, and is further accompanied by sediment the chelated clusters (Fig. 17). The linear range of Cu2+ detection was 0.02 µg/ ml to 1 µg/ml, with the LOD of 0.02 µg/ ml.

As illustrated in Fig. S16 (see supporting information), the overthrow ratio of the samples indicates a perfect linear relationship with various concentrations of Hg2+ in the range from 0.001 to 1 μg/ml. The limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed colorimetric method was measured by the equation of LOD = 3σ/m, in which, σ is the standard deviation, and m is the calibration curve slop [75]. With this regard, the LOD of our colorimetric sensing system is estimated as 0.001 ppm. These were lesser than the FDA measured level, in which the determined allowable points of inorganic mercury are 0.1 ppm and 0.001 ppm in infant formulas and drinking water, respectively [90].

Tables 1, and 2
list an indication of other approaches and the current strategy for Hg2+, and Cu2+ recognition, respectively. From the direct evaluation, it is obvious that our sensing system is outperformed than the traditional methods (Atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS), Atomic emission spectroscopy (AES), and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS)), and also is more sensitive than several nanomaterial-based techniques. Additionally, it is a simple, immediate, and cost-effective way of health-destructive ion sensing. In this work, our aim is to provide an analytical device for heavy metal ions recognition on the basis of CysA-AuNPs, and the pivotal aspects taken into account are accuracy, convenience, reliability, rapidity, and simplicity. Our outcomes also indicated that the dynamic recognition range is apparently wider than these literature values. Therefore, we expect this technique paves a novel way for subsequent works in biological studies, and environmental safety test.

Using AuNP-sCysA supported cysteine/methionine, in the presence of Cu2+, regardless of the cysteine/methionine concentration, the cysteine/methionine amino acid molecules are detached from the AuNPs-CysA and participate in the chelating process of Cu2+, owing to the chelating behavior of cysteine and methionine towards Cu2+. Therefore, the bind energy of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine/methionine are increased after cysteine/methionine decomposition in the presence of Cu2+. As the result, the UV–vis peak of AuNPs-CysA supported cysteine/methionine is raised in the presence of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in human real samples (urine)
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the obtained results in water-ion samples, the ion-modified- urine samples (Fig. S18 (see supporting information) confirmed the selectivity of the suggested nanoprobes for merely mercury ion, detecting both visual, and by UV–vis spectrometry. The obtained UV–vis spectra of CysA-AuNPs in exposure to Hg2+ ion (standard ion solution 1 μg/ml) and cysteine are represented in Fig. S18 (see supporting information). A remarkable change was observed in the UV − vis spectra of CysA-AuNPs in the presence of Hg2+, which on the other hand indicates an increment in the absorption UV − vis spectra of CysA-AuNPs at around 560 nm, confirming the aggregation of CysA-AuNPs. Therefore, the relevant colorimetric response is considerably enhanced, as can be observed in Fig. S18 (see supporting information). The reason is owing to the role of Hg2+ in preventing CysA-AuNPs agglomeration, leading to increase the inter band distances, as discussed in the previous section.

Consequently, various concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0.001 μg/ml to 1 μg/ml) were applied to human urine sample, and distinguished the sensitivity of the suggested biosensor. The representative UV–vis spectra illustrated in Fig. 18 (see supporting information) results indicated that the absorption of UV–vis spectra of AuNP-CysA remarkably deceased in the presence of even the lowest concentration of Hg2+ (0.01 μg/ml), and cysteine, as additive. As previously mentioned, compositing with cysteine caused to reduce the UV–Vis peak intensity of the AuNP-CysA because the energy bind of AuNP-CysA is decreased in the presence of cysteine. When Hg2+ is added to the system, cysteine molecules lose their electrostatic binds with AuNP-CysA to form new binds with Hg2+. More concentrations of Hg2+ caused to more cysteine molecules detachment from AuNP-CysA. Therefore, the width of the energy bind of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine has a direct relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ in the system, because of more cysteine detachment from AuNP-CysA in higher concentrations of Hg2+. Consequently, the UV–vis peak intensity of AuNP-CysA supported cysteine raised by increasing the concentration of Hg2+.

The detected amounts of Hg2+ ion with the proposed AuNP-CysA-cysteine probe is even lesser than the FDA approved allowable mercury ion concentrations (phenylmercuric nitrate) in human blood plasma (10 μg/ml). This indicates that our proposed biosensor can be a promising device for early diagnosis of not only several cancers, but also the other relevant diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective identification of Hg2+ using μPADs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S19 (see supporting information) reveals the optical micrographs for the Hg2+ detection assays performed on μPADs. As illustrated in Fig. 20, the optical images on the μPAD was captured under natural light (Fig. S19A), and UV light (Fig. S19B), in which the color alternations of the cysteine-treated CysA-AuNPs was evaluated through the μ-channels, both via capillary method, and non-capillary sensing. The reason might be due to the disruption of the AuNPs aggregation when interfering with Hg2+ ion, as described in the previous sections. After 2 min, it was revealed that Hg2+ was interpreted to the paper more than the other ions, as shown in Fig. S19C (under natural and UV light). Furthermore, the colorimetric sensing was performed by capillary method, in which the cysteine-treated CysA-AuNPs sensing probe was placed on the central part of the μPAD. As shown in Fig. S19D, the color changing in the presence of Hg2+ is different in compare with the color changing in the presence of the other ions. The reason for more adsorption of the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs probe solution in the presence of Hg2+ is attributed to the interactions between Hg2+ ion and cysteine, respect to the high thiophilicity of cysteine, thus, leading to deagglomeration of the CysA-AuNPs. Evident is the fact that the deagglomerated CysA-AuNPs have lower dimensions than the agglomerated CysA-AuNPs, leading to more adsorption capability to the TLC paper, than the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs alone, or cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs in the presence of the other metal ions, except Hg2+. Besides, the capillary method (Fig. S19D) represents the color alternation of the CysA-AuNPs prob to royal purple color in the presence of Hg2+, while all of the other ions are illustrated in pink, similar to that in cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs.

Moreover, Cu2+ can be detected on the µPADs by AuNP-CysA supported cysteine (Figure S20 (see supporting information), and AuNP-CysA supported methionine (Figure S21 (see supporting information)), by colorimetric sensing. In the presence of cysteine, or methionine, regardless of the concentrations of the amino acids, cysteine/methionine are attached to the AuNPs through electrostatic binds. Since these amino acids are chelator agents for Cu2+, the presence of this ion caused to detach cysteiene/methionine from AuNPs-CysA, leading to form Cu2+ chelated clusters on the paper, which is accompanied by an obvious color alternation from pink (for AuNP-CysA supported cysteine/ methionine) to yellow (for the sensing solution in the presence of Cu2+), both on the in situ approach and capillary method (Figure S20), and (Figure S21). The chelating ring for Cu2+ in the presence of cysteine is occurred by interactions between the thiol and amine groups of cysteine ring with Cu2+, and the chelating ring of Cu2+ in the presence of methionine is happened by interactions between the carboxyl and amine groups of methionine with Cu2+.

On μPADs, the partially aggregated CysA-cysteine, which possess lower dimensions can path through the TLC paper substrate, while the completely agglomerated CysA-cysteine in the presence of the cysteine, and other metal ions, except Hg2+ cannot easily go through the paper, and remained unabsorbed on the substrate.

The proposed mercury, and copper ion sensing system is valid for either human biological fluids or environmental samples, using for both biomedical applications, and environmental contamination usages. To the best of our knowledge, the proposed technique for selective Hg2+ and Cu2+ detection amongst 20 different metal ions in aqueous, human urine sample, and on paper-based μPADs is completely innovative and was not previously reported. The results revealed our new technique for the colorimetric recognition of mercury ion, in both urine and aqueous samples, is selective, simple, fast, sensitive, quantitative, cost-effective, and eco-friendly which can be detected by naked eye through color alternation with admirable stability.

Mercuric cation is a toxic ion that negatively affects animals, plants, and consequently, humans' health. Moreover, the accumulation of this toxic metal throughout the food chain drives hazardous health consequences in humans' body, such as brain damage, kidney failure, neuromuscular effects, and troubles in the digestive system. Hence, Hg2+ detection is pivotal for the health of the living organisms. An ion detecting colorimetric sensor based on amino acid-modified CysA-AuNPs was designed and successfully applied on biological and environmental samples. The cysteine treated CysA-AuNPs act as a colorimetric nanoprobe through aggregation due to the hydrogen bonding interplays with the mercury ion, causing to color alternation from wine red to royal pink that can be detected by naked eye. In the presence of the other ions, except Hg2+, CysA-AuNPs agglomeration can be triggered even more than the cysteine-supported probe without ions leading to change the color of the cysteine-supported CysA-AuNPs solution from royal purple to blue. Moreover, since cysteine and methionine are chelating agents for Cu2+, the addition of these amino acids to the AuNP-CysA solution caused to sense Cu2+. These amino acids make electrostatic interactions with AuNPs in concentration of 1 mM, which are detached form AuNP-CysA in the presence of Cu2+ to make chelating rings around Cu2+ through amine/thiol interactions, and amine/carboxyl interactions for cysteine, and methionine, respectively. This process is accompanied by a significant deagglomeration of the AuNPs-CysA which is accompanied by the color alternation of the sensing solution from pink to ruby red, and can be detected by the UV–vis spectra by raising the energy bind of AuNPs-CysA during cysteine/methionine detachment from AuNP-CysA.

Hg2+ ion detection was assessed via both UV–vis spectrophotometry, and colorimetric sensing. This fact is confirmed by TEM, and DLS in this work. Moreover, ATR-FTIR, SEM and EDS analysis revealed the elemental analysis of the prepared CysA-AuNPs system. We confirmed our results for Hg2+ and Cu2+ sensing on the home-made μPAD sensor, which successfully recognized the color alternation of the mercury ion both with capillary and non-capillary methods. Moreover, we proposed to apply amino acids, human biological substances, to recognize Hg2+ and Cu2+ with the sensitivity of 0.001 ppm, and 0.02 ppm, respectively. Thus, this proposed sensing probe can be efficiently useful for preventing the diseases which are raised from the Hg2+ and Cu2+ toxic effects in both environment and human body. In addition, the enhanced color uniformity, the low instrumental needs of the stamp, and disposability of μPADs enable the application of the suggested paper-based device for the commercial diagnostics biosensor.
